channel.title: DFIR Radar
channel.description: Keeping DFIR Intelligence on your Radar. The best DFIR research from hundreds of sources, filtered for relevance.

item.title: FBI Takes Down APT28 Network Behind Global DNS Hijacking Attacks
item.description:
FBI disrupts Russian 🇷🇺 APT28's global DNS hijacking network targeting thousands of TP-Link routers. Operation Masquerade neutralized malicious DNS resolvers used for credential harvesting via adversary-in-the-middle attacks.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041773654443233417

item.title: Iran-Linked Hackers Breach U.S. Industrial Systems, Trigger Disruptions
item.description:
Iranian 🇮🇷 APT actively breaches internet-facing PLCs to disrupt industrial operations, not just steal data. Attackers modify logic files and SCADA displays in real-time across water, energy, and government sectors.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041758572250308730

item.title: Introducing C2 Detection: Know When Your Edge Devices Are Calling Home to Attackers
item.description:

item.description:

item.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
item.description:
Russian 🇷🇺 GRU hackers exploited vulnerable routers to mass-harvest Microsoft Office authentication tokens from 18,000+ networks. Forest Blizzard (APT28) used DNS hijacking to intercept OAuth tokens without deploying malware, affecting 200+ organizations.
Technical breakdown: • Targeted end-of-life SOHO routers (primarily MikroTik, TP-Link) using known vulnerabilities • Modified router DNS settings to redirect traffic through attacker-controlled servers • Intercepted OAuth tokens post-authentication, bypassing MFA entirely (T1557.001) • Peak activity December 2025: 18,000 routers, 5,000 consumer devices compromised • Primary targets: government ministries, law enforcement, third-party email providers
Attack chain: • Router exploitation → DNS server modification → network-wide DNS poisoning • Users authenticate normally to Microsoft services • Tokens automatically intercepted via adversary-in-the-middle on TLS connections • Direct account access without credential theft or phishing
DFIR artifacts: Monitor DNS server changes on edge devices, unusual OAuth token requests from unfamiliar IP ranges, and TLS certificate anomalies on Microsoft domains.
Hunt for recent DNS configuration changes on SOHO devices and correlate with suspicious OAuth activity in Microsoft 365 logs.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041592523177472286

item.title: Milking the last drop of Intego - Time for Windows to get its LPE
item.description:
Local Privilege Escalation in Intego Windows security software exploits file deletion feature to achieve SYSTEM privileges through Config.msi deletion primitive and directory junction abuse.
Technical details: • CVE pending - affects Intego 3.0.0.1 Windows version • IavService.exe (SYSTEM) deletes "duplicate" files without validating file types or reparse points • Vulnerable functions: IavFileDeleteEx_DeleteFileEx and IavFilesUtil_RemoveFile lack FILE_ATTRIBUTE_REPARSE_POINT checks • Time-of-check-time-of-use (TOCTOU) race condition between GetFileAttributesW() and DeleteFileW()
Attack chain: • Create duplicate files, initiate Intego optimization scan • Replace target file with NT object directory junction to \RPC Control during cleanup window • Create NT symlink at \RPC Control\deleteme2.txt → C:\Config.msi • std::filesystem::remove() fallback follows symlink, deletes Config.msi as SYSTEM • Standard Config.msi rollback technique drops HID.DLL for SYSTEM shell via osk.exe
Monitor for unexpected Config.msi deletion events and NT object manager operations by security software processes. Hunt for SYSTEM file operations on junction/symlink targets outside expected paths.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041577568529478080

item.title: SOHO router compromise leads to DNS hijacking and adversary-in-the-middle attacks
item.description:
Russian 🇷🇺 military intelligence unit Forest Blizzard compromises 200+ organizations and 5,000 consumer devices through large-scale SOHO router exploitation for DNS hijacking and adversary-in-the-middle attacks.
Campaign details: • Storm-2754 sub-group exploits vulnerable home/small office routers, modifies DNS settings to redirect traffic through actor-controlled resolvers using dnsmasq utility • TLS AiTM attacks target Microsoft Outlook web domains and government servers in Africa 🌍, intercepting plaintext traffic including emails when users ignore certificate warnings • Attack chain: Router compromise → DNS hijacking via DHCP manipulation → selective AiTM against high-value targets for intelligence collection • Threat actor presents invalid TLS certificates to victims, requiring user interaction to complete compromise • Campaign active since August 2025, impacts government, IT, telecom, and energy sectors
Hunt for unusual DNS setting modifications on Windows endpoints and correlate with risky sign-ins. Monitor CloudAppEvents for "MailItemsAccessed" actions from suspicious accounts.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041562317700071558

item.title: API Authentication Bypass in FortiClient EMS 7.4.5-7.4.6–CVE-2026-35616
item.description:
Critical authentication bypass in FortiClient EMS 7.4.5-7.4.6 allows complete endpoint fleet takeover through HTTP header spoofing. Bishop Fox confirms active exploitation in the wild.
• CVE-2026-35616 (no CVSS provided) bypasses certificate auth via spoofed X-SSL-CLIENT-VERIFY and X-SSL-CLIENT-CERT headers • Django middleware trusts user-controllable headers as equivalent to Apache mod_ssl WSGI variables • Certificate validation performs only DN string matching - no cryptographic signature verification required • Attacker gains authenticated API access to quarantine endpoints, rotate JWT secrets, download ZTNA keys, export vulnerability data • Affects complete endpoint management infrastructure - FortiClient EMS controls organization's entire endpoint fleet • 16 cert_chain_approved endpoints across 15 controllers exposed to unauthenticated access
Fortinet hotfix strips malicious headers at Apache layer via RequestHeader unset directives. Code-level fix expected in v7.4.7.
Hunt for unusual EMS API access patterns and verify patching status with Bishop Fox's non-destructive scanner tool.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041547418869887195

item.title: The Unknown Stealers: What’s Hidden Below the Radar
item.description:
New research reveals lesser-known stealers like Void, Datura, and Saturn are operating below detection thresholds while feeding logs to ransomware groups. Void uses Steam profiles as C2 resolvers and dynamic syscalls to evade EDR hooks.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041532223824822695

item.title: APT28 exploit routers to enable DNS hijacking operations
item.description:
Russian 🇷🇺 APT28/GRU exploits SOHO routers for large-scale DNS hijacking campaign, targeting authentication credentials through adversary-in-the-middle attacks. NCSC UK attributes operations to Military Intelligence Unit 26165.
Campaign mechanics: • Exploited TP-Link WR841N routers via CVE-2023-50224 to modify DHCP DNS settings (T1584.008, T1588.006) • Compromised routers redirect DNS queries for email/login domains to actor-controlled infrastructure • Conducted AitM attacks against browser sessions and desktop applications to harvest passwords and OAuth tokens (T1557, T1586) • Opportunistic targeting with filtering at each stage to identify high-value intelligence targets
Infrastructure details: • Two distinct IP clusters containing 150+ malicious DNS servers and AitM infrastructure • Primary DNS set to malicious IPs, secondary to legitimate servers for stealth • Interactive operations against MikroTik routers in Ukraine 🇺🇦 for intelligence collection • Multiple router models targeted beyond TP-Link WR841N
Hunt for unexpected DNS server changes in DHCP configurations, monitor for authentication traffic to suspicious IPs, and audit router firmware versions against CVE-2023-50224. Full IOC list with 150+ malicious IPs in the advisory.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041501907273851005

item.title: What we learned about TEE security from auditing WhatsApp's Private Inference
item.description:
Trail of Bits audit of WhatsApp's TEE-based AI feature found 28 vulnerabilities including 8 critical flaws that could bypass privacy protections. Key lesson: every unmeasured input becomes an attack vector in TEE deployments.

item.title: The Trojan horse of cybercrime: Weaponizing SaaS notification pipelines
item.description:

#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041456546719093008

item.title: Common Entra ID Security Assessment Findings – Part 3: Weak Privileged Identity Management Configuration
item.description:
New research reveals critical weaknesses in Microsoft Entra ID Privileged Identity Management configurations that attackers can exploit to bypass MFA requirements and maintain persistent privileged access.
Compass Security identifies widespread PIM misconfigurations during assessments:
• **Token Abuse Scenarios**: Attackers with stolen refresh tokens can wait for legitimate users to activate privileged roles, then use FOCI capabilities to obtain elevated access tokens without additional MFA challenges
• **Built-in Azure MFA Bypass**: Stolen tokens containing MFA claims can activate roles directly via PowerShell using Microsoft Graph API calls to `/roleManagement/directory/roleAssignmentScheduleRequests`
• **Authentication Context Limitation**: 5-minute clock skew window allows role activation even with "every time" reauthentication requirements - attackers can exploit fresh tokens within this timeframe
• **Common Misconfigurations**: Permanent assignments allowed, excessive 4+ hour activation durations, missing notifications, and high-privilege roles (Global Admin, Security Admin) not protected by approval workflows
• **Detection Gaps**: Organizations often assume PIM provides complete protection while neglecting Conditional Access policies for device/location restrictions
Hunt for suspicious PIM activations outside business hours and unusual API calls to Graph endpoints. Full technical details and EntraFalcon detection tool available in the research.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041426441867350019

item.title: Delivered by Trust: What the Axios Supply Chain Attack… | Bishop Fox
item.description:
Supply chain compromise: Axios NPM package backdoored with platform-specific RATs affecting versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4. Attackers gained initial access through social engineering of trusted maintainer account.
Technical breakdown: • Malicious versions contain trojanized dependency executing setup.js loader during installation • Second-stage connects to sfrclak[.]com and 142[.]11[.]206[.]73:8000 for RAT delivery • Attack chain: compromised maintainer → malicious NPM publish → dependency execution → C2 → OS-specific payload • IOCs include plain-crypto-js package and [email protected]/[email protected] dependencies • Impacts entire development pipeline from workstations to production systems
Hunt priorities: • Scan for affected Axios versions (1.14.1, 0.30.4) across all environments • Check CI/CD logs for npm install execution during compromise window • Monitor network connections to identified C2 infrastructure • Validate credential rotation on all affected systems
Roll back to safe versions (1.14.0, 0.30.3), clear node_modules and NPM cache, then implement dependency pinning and SBOM monitoring.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041306546110697552

item.title: 50,000 WordPress Sites affected by Arbitrary File Upload Vulnerability in Ninja Forms – File Upload WordPress Plugin
item.description:
Critical arbitrary file upload flaw in Ninja Forms File Upload plugin (CVE-2026-0740, CVSS 9.8) affects 50,000 WordPress sites. Unauthenticated attackers can achieve RCE by uploading malicious PHP files. Update to version 3.3.27 immediately.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041290490788655489

item.title: Phishing LNK files and GitHub C2 power new DPRK cyber attacks
item.description:
North Korean 🇰🇵 threat actors target South Korean 🇰🇷 organizations using GitHub as C2 infrastructure. Campaign leverages obfuscated LNK files dropping decoy PDFs while silently executing PowerShell scripts for surveillance operations.
Technical breakdown: • LNK files embed decoding functions with XOR keys, drop decoy PDFs titled "Hangul Document" while executing PowerShell (T1566.001, T1059.001) • PowerShell performs environment checks, creates scheduled task persistence, exfiltrates system data to private GitHub repos (T1053.005, T1041) • Multiple GitHub accounts identified: motoralis (primary hub), God0808RAMA, Pigresy80, entire73, pandora0009, brandonleeodd93-blip • Keep-alive script uploads network configs to hxxps://api[.]github[.]com/repos/motoralis/singled/contents/jjyun/network/ using PUT method • Campaign active since 2024, evolved from simple obfuscation to sophisticated encoding techniques
Forensic artifacts: LNK file metadata analysis, PowerShell execution logs, scheduled tasks in Task Scheduler, temp folder artifacts. Monitor GitHub API calls and unusual PowerShell/VBScript activity targeting Korean orgs.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041275622572359765

item.title: jq For Forensics – Righteous IT
item.description:

#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041247303441256533

item.title: Inside an AI‑enabled device code phishing campaign
item.description:
Microsoft 🇺🇸 exposes AI-powered device code phishing campaign using dynamic code generation and Railway.com infrastructure to bypass 15-minute OAuth expiration windows. Campaign shows significant evolution from Storm-2372 with automated backend systems and hyper-personalized lures targeting organizational accounts at scale.
Key technical details: • Dynamic device code generation via /api/device/start/ endpoint with X-Antibot-Token header bypasses static code limitations • Backend automation on Railway.com, Vercel, Cloudflare Workers creates thousands of short-lived polling nodes • AI-generated personalized phishing emails targeting financial/executive roles with RFP, invoice, manufacturing themes • Clipboard hijacking via navigator.clipboard.writeText API auto-copies device codes for faster user authentication • Post-compromise: Microsoft Graph reconnaissance, device registration for Primary Refresh Token persistence, malicious inbox rules
Attack chain methodology: • Phase 1: GetCredentialType endpoint validation 10-15 days before phishing • Phase 2-3: Multi-stage redirects through compromised domains to final landing page with browser-in-browser technique • Phase 4: Real-time polling every 3-5 seconds via checkStatus() function during 15-minute window • Phase 5-6: Token validation, device registration, Graph API organizational mapping, targeted email exfiltration
DFIR artifacts and detection opportunities:
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041230220850561297

item.title: CVE-2026-35616: FortiClient EMS API Auth Bypass Enables Command Execution
item.description:
CVE-2026-35616 in FortiClient EMS enables unauthenticated API bypass leading to command execution (CVSS 9.1). Fortinet confirms active exploitation since March 31st. Apply hotfix immediately for versions 7.4.5-7.4.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041215251882885186

item.title: Storm-1175 focuses gaze on vulnerable web-facing assets in high-tempo Medusa ransomware operations
item.description:
Storm-1175 ransomware group weaponizes N-day vulnerabilities within 24-48 hours of disclosure, targeting healthcare, education, and finance sectors. Actor exploits 16+ CVEs since 2023 and deploys Medusa ransomware in under one day.
Key findings: • Exploited vulnerabilities include Exchange (CVE-2023-21529), TeamCity (CVE-2024-27198/27199), ConnectWise ScreenConnect (CVE-2024-1709/1708), and emerging threats like SAP NetWeaver (CVE-2025-31324) • Uses zero-day exploits 1 week before disclosure (CVE-2026-23760 SmarterMail, CVE-2025-10035 GoAnywhere MFT) • Post-compromise: Creates admin accounts, deploys RMM tools (Atera, SimpleHelp, ConnectWise), uses PDQ Deployer for lateral movement • Credential theft via LSASS dumping, Mimikatz, NTDS.dit access from Domain Controllers • Data exfiltration with Rclone, disables Windows Defender via registry tampering before deploying Medusa ransomware
Organizations should implement external attack surface monitoring, enable Credential Guard, turn on tamper protection, and deploy attack surface reduction rules blocking LSASS credential theft and PSExec lateral movement.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041200007349752185

item.title: Pro-Iran Cyberattacks on Financial Services: 144 Incidents
item.description:
Iranian 🇮🇷-linked coalition escalates cyber campaign against global financial services with 144 verified incidents across 14 countries. Attack methodology shifts from DDoS disruption in March to destructive wipers in April, targeting banks as economic warfare proxies.
Campaign details: • Financial services ranked target (144/1,583 incidents) behind government (486), spanning Israeli 🇮🇱 banks to Gulf states and African 🇰🇪🇷🇼🇲🇦 financial ministries • 70+ hacktivist groups plus Russian 🇷🇺 NoName057(16), Iraqi 🇮🇶 313 Team, coordinated with state APT MuddyWater pre-positioning access • April escalation: destructive attacks jumped from 0.4% to 16.7% of incidents, with Anonymous For Justice debuting 8 destructive hits on Israeli 🇮🇱 financial firms • MuddyWater deployed Dindoor/Fakeset Python backdoors in US 🇺🇸 bank before campaign launch, using GitHub/Google Drive/Telegram for C2 (T1102) • Stryker-scale threat: March wiper attack abused Microsoft Intune to destroy 200k+ devices across 79 countries via remote wipe commands
Hunt for unauthorized GitHub/Google Drive connections from non-dev endpoints, audit MDM admin access, and treat ransomware as potential destruction-focused rather than profit-driven during this conflict period.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041187788260069582

item.title: Hackers Are Attempting to Turn ComfyUI Servers Into a Cryptomining Proxy Botnet
item.description:
Active campaign exploits ComfyUI AI servers via malicious custom nodes to deploy cryptominers and proxy botnets. Over 1,000 exposed instances targeted through automated Python scanner that achieves RCE on unauthenticated deployments.
Key technical details: • Exploits FL_CodeNode, EvaluateMultiple, and SrlEval custom nodes for arbitrary Python execution (T1059.006) • If no vulnerable nodes present, installs malicious ComfyUI-Shell-Executor package via pip endpoint • Downloads ghost.sh payload using memfd_create for fileless execution and LD_PRELOAD rootkit • Deploys XMRig (Monero) and lolMiner (Conflux) with process masquerading as khugepaged_<hex> and nv_uvm_<hex> • Triple persistence: inotify guard process, scattered filesystem copies, and v8.2 backdoors (fake GPU monitor node + poisoned default workflow)
C2 infrastructure: • Flask dashboard at 77[.]110[.]96[.]200:3301 (admin/pickmezr) manages mining fleet and Hysteria v2 proxy network • Targets major cloud providers (AWS, GCP, Oracle) with continuous 3-4 hour scan cycles
Hunt for ComfyUI instances with /object_info endpoints containing FL_CodeNode or EvaluateMultiple. Monitor for khugepaged/nv_uvm processes with hex suffixes and outbound connections to Kryptex mining pools.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2041169845178466360

item.title: Uffizi cyberattack: BabLock TTPs, IOCs and attribution gaps
item.description:
BabLock ransomware analysis following Uffizi Galleries 🇮🇹 attack reveals 4.5-minute encryption capability via notepad.exe injection and GPO propagation. Eastern European attribution suspected but unconfirmed.
Technical breakdown: • Initial access via Zimbra exploitation (T1190), followed by DLL side-loading into signed executables (T1574.002) • Process injection into notepad.exe with direct syscalls to evade user-mode monitoring (T1055, T1106) • Domain Controller compromise enables GPO creation for enterprise-wide distribution (T1484.001) • Aggressive recovery impairment: shadow copy deletion, service stops, log clearing (T1490, T1489, T1070.001) • IOCs: cy.exe, winutils.dll, _r_e_a_d_m_e.txt, k1k2k3 extensions with 00-99 suffixes
Also linked to La Sapienza University 🇮🇹 attack. Critical detection window is domain privilege escalation—once achieved, full enterprise encryption occurs within minutes.
Hunt for notepad.exe with network connections, unexpected DLL loads by signed binaries, and unauthorized GPO creation. Full TTP mapping and defensive countermeasures in the analysis.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2040414611854512329

item.title: Six Accounts, One Actor: Inside the prt-scan Supply Chain Campaign
item.description:
AI-powered campaign exploits GitHub pull_request_target workflows across 500+ malicious PRs, successfully compromising npm packages and stealing cloud credentials. Attack spans six waves from March-April 2026, evolving from basic scripts to sophisticated AI-generated payloads.
Key technical details: • Exploits pull_request_target misconfiguration for repository secret access (T1195.002) • Branch naming: prt-scan-{12-hex-chars}, consistent "ci: update build configuration" PR titles • Multi-language injection: conftest.py, package.json, build.rs, Makefile targeting based on repo stack • Five-phase payload: EXFIL (env dump), RECON (API enumeration), DISPATCH (workflow creation), LABEL_BYPASS (gate evasion), DELAYED (/proc scanning) • Confirmed compromise of @codfish/eslint-config and @codfish/actions npm packages across 106 versions
Attack methodology: • AI-assisted targeting at 7 PRs/hour sustained rate with adaptive framework detection • Base64-encoded exfiltration via workflow logs and PR comments for persistence • Cloud metadata probing for AWS/Azure/GCP SSRF opportunities • Stolen credentials include AWS keys, Cloudflare API tokens, Netlify auth tokens
DFIR artifacts: • GitHub audit logs for pull_request_target workflow triggers from external contributors • Workflow execution logs containing base64-encoded environment dumps • PR comments with ==PRT_DELAYED_START_{NONCE}== markers containing compressed secrets
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2040369392207233314

item.title: Supply Chain Attacks Surge in March 2026
item.description:
Supply chain attacks surge: North Korean 🇰🇵 actors compromise Axios NPM package, TeamPCP group targets AI infrastructure via malicious PyPI packages. Five major attacks hit critical developer tools in March 2026 alone.
Key incidents: • Axios compromise: Account takeover bypassed CI/CD, injected plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 dependency with cross-platform RAT targeting Windows/macOS/Linux via sfrclak[.]com C2 • LiteLLM attack: TeamPCP published malicious v1.82.7-1.82.8 with credential harvesting payloads targeting AWS/GCP/Azure tokens and SSH keys (3.4M daily downloads affected) • Attack vectors: Maintainer account takeovers, .pth file abuse for Python startup execution, postinstall scripts in package.json dependencies • Additional targets: Trivy scanner, KICS analysis tool, Telnyx communication library all compromised by TeamPCP group • Detection gaps: Malicious packages passed hash verification, remained live 3+ hours before quarantine
Hunt for unexpected dependencies in package.json/requirements.txt, connections to checkmarx[.]zone or models[.]litellm[.]cloud, and .pth files in Python site-packages directories.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2040233677578285310

item.title: Supply Chain Attacks Surge in March 2026
item.description:
Major NPM and PyPI supply chain attacks hit critical packages in March 2026. North Korean 🇰🇵 actors compromised Axios (1.6M+ daily downloads) while TeamPCP group poisoned AI library LiteLLM, both targeting developer credentials and CI/CD systems.
Key attacks analyzed: • **Axios compromise**: Account takeover bypassed CI/CD, injected plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 dependency with cross-platform RAT dropper contacting sfrclak[.]com (142.11.206[.]73) • **LiteLLM attack**: TeamPCP deployed credential harvester via .pth file (v1.82.8) and Base64 payload (v1.82.7) targeting AWS/GCP/Azure tokens, SSH keys, K8s creds • **Affected versions**: axios@1.14.1, axios@0.30.4, litellm@1.82.7-1.82.8 • **Attack duration**: LiteLLM packages quarantined within 3 hours; Axios discovered March 30
DFIR artifacts: • Package.json modifications, postinstall script execution logs • Network connections to C2 domains: sfrclak[.]com, checkmarx[.]zone, models[.]litellm[.]cloud • File system indicators: litellm_init.pth in site-packages/, modified proxy_server.py
Hunt for recent NPM/PyPI installs with network beaconing and review lockfiles for compromised package versions. Full IOC hashes and detection signatures available.

item.title: Axois NPM Supply Chain Incident
item.description:
Supply chain attack on Axios npm package exposed 100 million weekly downloads to platform-specific RATs. Malicious versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 deployed March 31, 2026 for 3-hour window before detection.
Technical breakdown: • Fake dependency "plain-crypto-js" executes post-install, contacts 142[.]11[.]206[.]73 for OS-specific payload delivery • Windows: Downloads PS1, copies powershell.exe to %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe, executes with bypass flags • MacOS: Deploys "com.apple.act.mond" binary via zsh execution • Linux: Python-based RAT with remote access capabilities • Attack vector: Supply chain compromise (T1195.002) with automatic payload deployment
DFIR teams should immediately: - Hunt for plain-crypto-js dependency in package.json/node_modules - Monitor for network connections to 142[.]11[.]206[.]73 and sfrclak[.]com - Check %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe on Windows systems - Scan for the provided SHA256 hashes across endpoints - Rotate all credentials on affected systems
Organizations using Axios should rollback to v1.14.0/v0.30.3 and assume credential compromise if malicious versions were downloaded. Full IOC list available in Cisco Talos report.

item.title: Simplifying MBA obfuscation with CoBRA
item.description:
New open-source tool CoBRA simplifies Mixed Boolean-Arithmetic (MBA) obfuscation with 99.86% success rate across 73,000+ expressions. Addresses critical gap where algebraic simplifiers can't handle bitwise logic and Boolean minimizers fail on arithmetic.
Key capabilities: • Handles full MBA spectrum: linear, semilinear, polynomial, and mixed expressions using worklist-based orchestrator with 36 discrete passes • Three deployment modes: CLI tool, C++ library, and LLVM pass plugin for integration into existing analysis pipelines • Worklist classifies expressions and routes to appropriate simplification technique (pattern matching, ANF conversion, interpolation) • Verification engine uses random input testing or Z3 theorem proving to confirm every simplification before output • Successfully processes expressions like "(x&y)+(x|y)" → "x+y" and complex nested obfuscations
DFIR practitioners can integrate CoBRA into automated malware analysis workflows to recover readable code from MBA-obfuscated samples. Available on GitHub under Apache 2.0 license.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2040067567684133352

item.title: Simplifying MBA obfuscation with CoBRA
item.description:
Trail of Bits releases CoBRA, an open-source tool that simplifies Mixed Boolean-Arithmetic (MBA) obfuscation with 99.86% success rate across 73,000+ expressions. The tool bridges the gap between algebraic and Boolean simplification techniques.
Key capabilities: • Handles full spectrum of MBA obfuscation: linear, semilinear, polynomial, and mixed expressions used by malware authors and packers • Uses worklist-based orchestrator with 36 discrete passes across four families to classify and route expressions appropriately • Transforms complex obfuscated code like "((a^b)|(a^c)) + 65469 * ~((a&(b&c))) + 65470 * (a&(b&c))" into readable "67 + (a | b | c)" • Ships as CLI tool, C++ library, and LLVM pass plugin for integration into existing analysis pipelines • Validates all results through random input testing or Z3 theorem proving before returning simplified expressions
Three deployment modes enable flexible integration: direct CLI usage with bitwidth specification, C++ library linking for custom tools, and LLVM pass for deobfuscating IR patterns across multiple basic blocks.
Essential for reverse engineers tackling MBA-protected malware, VM-based obfuscators, and commercial software protection schemes. Available on GitHub under Apache 2.0 license.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2040052395967697285

item.title: Analysis of cifrat: could this be an evolution of a mobile RAT?
item.description:

Technical breakdown: • Initial vector: Phishing SMS directing to fake Booking.com update site hosting malicious APK • Stage 1: Dropper APK requests accessibility permissions, downloads encrypted payload • Stage 2: Hidden RAT gains full device control via Android Accessibility API abuse • C2 uses WebSocket protocol for real-time command execution and data exfiltration • Capabilities include screen capture, keylogging, SMS theft, and remote device manipulation
DFIR artifacts: • APK installation logs in /data/system/packages.list and package manager events • Accessibility service registrations in Android system logs • WebSocket network connections to suspicious domains • Encrypted payload remnants in app data directories
Hunt for apps requesting accessibility permissions without legitimate business need, especially those with network activity to non-standard ports or WebSocket protocols.

item.title: The Axios npm supply chain incident: fake dependency, real backdoor
item.description:
Axios npm supply chain attack: Compromised maintainer account pushed trojanized versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 with cross-platform RAT payloads. Malicious dependency "plain-crypto-js" deployed backdoors on developer workstations and Docker containers before removal.
Key technical details: • Malicious versions injected plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 dependency with postinstall dropper (setup.js) • Windows payload: PowerShell binary copied to C:\ProgramData\wt.exe, triggers MDE alert "System executable renamed and launched" • Multi-stage execution: VBS script downloads 6202033.ps1, establishes persistence via Registry Run key "MicrosoftUpdate" • Cross-platform RAT capabilities: system recon, remote command execution, file enumeration (T1082, T1083, T1059) • C2 infrastructure: sfrclak[.]com:8000, callnrwise[.]com, calltan[.]com with consistent "6202033" naming convention
Attack chain: npm install → postinstall script → OS detection → payload download → persistence setup via Registry Run key → C2 communication
DFIR artifacts: • Registry key: HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\MicrosoftUpdate • File paths: C:\ProgramData\wt.exe, C:\ProgramData\system.bat, 6202033.vbs/ps1 • Network indicators: Outbound connections to C2 domains on port 8000
Hunt for node.exe spawning file creation events for wt.exe or powershell.exe, and Registry Run key modifications with "MicrosoftUpdate" value. Full KQL queries and IOCs provided in the report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2040022167639773350

item.title: BLACKNET-00: The Ransomware-as-a-Service Platform That Weaponizes Mediocrity
item.description:
BLACKNET-00 ransomware builder drops technical barriers to near-zero: $500 GUI platform enables script kiddies to deploy enterprise-grade ransomware with AES-256/RSA encryption, DGA C2, and anti-analysis evasion.
Platform weaponizes mediocrity at scale: • Hybrid encryption stack (AES-256, RSA, ChaCha20) with custom ransom notes breaks traditional YARA detection • Multi-layered evasion: VM/sandbox detection (T1497), delayed execution, DLL hijacking persistence (T1574.001) • Tor-backed Domain Generation Algorithm C2 defeats IP blocking and DNS sinkholing • Built-in data exfiltration targets crypto wallets, credentials, webcam footage for double extortion • Self-propagation via SMB lateral movement and USB spreading transforms endpoints into vectors
DFIR implications severe: Each payload uniquely configured, making hash-based detection obsolete. Anti-VM features actively degrade sandbox analysis, compressing IOC dissemination windows. DGA C2 requires ML-based DNS anomaly detection over static blocklists.
Threat landscape shift imminent: Expect surge in SMB/mid-market targeting with lower ransom demands but exponentially higher volume. 149% YoY ransomware increase already documented in Q1 2025.
Hunt for rapid file rename operations, shadow copy deletion, and DLL loads from non-standard paths. Prioritize behavioral EDR over signatures.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039840992304550294

item.title: Inside TeamPCP’s Shell Arsenal
item.description:
TeamPCP's shell arsenal analyzed: 23 bash scripts used across massive supply chain attacks targeting GitHub, PyPI, and Kubernetes environments. Campaign active December 2025-March 2026, compromising Trivy, Checkmarx, LiteLLM with multi-stage credential harvesting.
Technical breakdown: • 10 shells in Trivy attack: ranging from 179B credential stealer to 17KB multi-stage loaders • Memory scraping via /proc/*/mem for GitHub tokens, AWS keys, crypto wallets • Kubectl auto-installation + Python payload delivery (kube.py, prop.py) for K8s lateral movement • Steganography: payloads hidden in .wav files with Base64 encoding • Hybrid encryption: AES-256-CBC + RSA-4096 for data exfiltration to typo-squatted domains • Self-propagating worm capabilities via systemd persistence (teampcp-react.service)
Attack chain artifacts: • Compressed loot in tpcp.tar.gz archives • Fallback exfil via victim's own GitHub repos (tpcp-docs) • C2 channels: typo-squatted domains, Cloudflare tunnels, ICP blockchain, GitHub dead-drops • MITRE TTPs: T1070 (indicator removal), T1564 (hide artifacts), T1573 (encrypted channel)
Hunt for /proc/*/mem access by non-root processes, kubectl downloads to /tmp, and outbound connections to .trycloudflare.com tunnels. Full IOC list with 23 MD5 hashes and PGP key in report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039826059772805429

item.title: Spyrtacus and the fake WhatsApp client behind a hidden surveillance campaign
item.description:
Italian 🇮🇹 lawful interception vendor Asigint deployed Spyrtacus spyware against 200+ WhatsApp users through fake mobile clients. Meta publicly attributed the campaign, revealing social engineering tactics using telecom-branded lures across Android, Windows, and iOS platforms.
Technical breakdown: • Delivery via targeted SMS impersonating TIM, Vodafone, WINDTRE carriers leading to cloned download pages • Fake WhatsApp clients request accessibility permissions for overlay attacks and data exfiltration (T1444, T1432) • Capabilities: SMS/call interception, contact harvesting, audio/video capture, location tracking (T1412, T1429, T1512, T1430) • Campaign active since 2018, shifted from Play Store to dedicated Italian 🇮🇹 distribution sites after Google hardening • C2 infrastructure registration metadata links to ASIGINT/SIO ecosystem per TechCrunch analysis
DFIR artifacts: • Package names impersonating official WhatsApp/carrier apps with non-Play Store install sources • Accessibility service abuse for legitimate communication apps • Timeline correlation between suspicious SMS, browser visits, and sideload installations • iOS: untrusted provisioning profiles and unexpected device management entries
Hunt for apps with accessibility permissions that don't match their stated purpose, correlate SMS timestamps with suspicious app installs, and baseline normal carrier communication patterns.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039810850848969088

item.title: Securing the Supply Chain: How SentinelOne®’s AI EDR Stops the Axios Attack Autonomously
item.description:
North Korean 🇰🇵 state actors hijacked npm credentials for Axios HTTP client, deploying cross-platform RAT to 600,000 downloads in 3-hour window. Attack bypassed OIDC Trusted Publishing by exploiting legacy token coexistence.
Key technical details: • Malicious packages: axios@1.14.1/0.30.4 with plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 dependency containing WAVESHAPER.V2 RAT • C2 infrastructure: sfrclak[.]com (142.11.206[.]73) with HTTP beacons every 60s, Base64+XOR obfuscation • Multi-platform payloads: %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe (Windows), /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond (macOS), /tmp/ld.py (Linux) • Renamed binary execution technique (T1036.003) - PowerShell copied to wt.exe for evasion • Self-deletion after deployment, leaving forensic evidence only in lockfiles and audit logs
Attack methodology: • UNC1069/Sapphire Sleet compromised maintainer credentials and exploited npm's authentication priority logic • Pre-staged clean package 18 hours before attack to evade novelty detection • Postinstall hook silently deployed RAT targeting npm tokens, SSH keys, CI/CD secrets • Operational timing: Sunday midnight UTC to maximize response window before detection
DFIR artifacts and detection: • Package-lock.json/yarn.lock references to plain-crypto-js indicate exposure even after self-deletion • DNS queries to sfrclak[.]com and connections to 142.11.206[.]73:8000 • Anachronistic User-Agent: "mozilla/4.0 (compatible; msie 8.0; windows nt 5.1; trident/4.0)"
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039795767796175305

item.title: Cutting Through the Noise: A Technique-Based Approach to Hunting Web-Delivered Malware
item.description:
Censys researchers develop scalable technique-based hunting methodology that reduces entire observable web to 42 actionable results, discovering live ClickFix campaign on compromised Turkish 🇹🇷 medical site delivering XWorm V5.6 via PhantomVAI MaaS loader.
Key technical details: • Query combines PowerShell + obfuscation indicators (atob(), eval(), navigator.clipboard) with layered negation filtering across 200+ patterns • Attack chain: ClickFix page → HTA with Unicode emoji obfuscation → steganographic JPEG on archive.org → PhantomVAI loader → XWorm V5.6 • C2: 86.106.85[.]194:9000 with self-signed cert CN=WIN-3O8L0TLB135 • Static payload recovery achieved without sandbox - PhantomVAI uses simple string reversal + base64 (no crypto) • XWorm config AES-encrypted with MD5(mutex) key: UGUHrsQaOFvrEntr
Attack methodology: • Compromised orcanmedikal[.]com[.]tr serves identical ClickFix pages across subdomains (body hash c52314cea...) • PowerShell -EncodedCommand launches mshta.exe with -Verb RunAs for UAC bypass • 5-stage chain uses steganographic hiding in JPEG images, process hollowing into RegAsm.exe • PhantomVAI performs VM detection before deploying commodity RATs (T1497, T1055.012)
Hunt for mshta.exe spawning PowerShell processes, RegAsm.exe with network connections, and navigator.clipboard API calls in web responses. Full query structure and IOC list available in report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039780729874161945

item.title: Cookie-controlled PHP webshells: A stealthy tradecraft in Linux hosting environments
item.description:
Microsoft uncovers cookie-controlled PHP webshells targeting Linux hosting environments. Threat actors use HTTP cookies as execution gates, keeping malicious code dormant during normal traffic and activating only when specific cookie conditions are met.
Key technical details: • Webshells parse $_COOKIE superglobal for execution triggers instead of URL parameters or POST bodies • Multi-stage loaders use base64 decoding + runtime function reconstruction (eval, include, system calls built character-by-character) • Persistence via cron jobs that recreate webshells if deleted: "echo <blob> | base64 -d > file.php" patterns • Self-healing mechanism through scheduled tasks in cPanel/jailshell environments (T1053.003) • Advanced variants segment cookie data into function identifiers, file paths, and decoding routines
Attack methodology: • Initial access through compromised hosting accounts or web app exploitation (T1190) • Deploy obfuscated PHP loaders to web-accessible directories via web processes (php-fpm spawning shells) • Establish persistence through legitimate control panel cron scheduling • Cookie-gated activation reduces logging visibility while maintaining RCE capability
DFIR artifacts and detection opportunities: • Monitor web server processes (php-fpm, httpd, nginx) spawning shell interpreters (bash, sh, dash) • Hunt for base64 decode operations writing .php files: ProcessCommandLine contains "base64" AND ".php"
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039765551061344695

item.title: New Whitepaper: Stealthy BPFDoor Variants are a Needle That Looks Like Hay
item.description:
Rapid7 uncovers 7 new BPFDoor variants targeting global telecom infrastructure with kernel-level Berkeley Packet Filter evasion. Advanced httpShell and icmpShell variants use stateless C2 and ICMP relay to bypass enterprise security stacks.
Key technical findings: • httpShell uses "magic ruler" padding (26th byte offset) to survive WAF interference and kernel-level decapsulation for GRE/GTP tunnels • icmpShell implements PID-bound mutation with dynamic BPF filters, making static firewall rules ineffective (T1205, T1095) • Hidden IP (HIP) field with -1 flag enables stateless C2 - malware sends reverse shell to magic packet source IP • Variant H actively beacons to ntpussl.instanthq.com masquerading as NTP-over-SSL traffic on port 443 • Multi-threaded variant G spawns separate threads for TCP/UDP/ICMP protocol monitoring with triple-redundant packet capture
Detection opportunities: • Monitor AF_PACKET socket creation with SOCK_RAW/SOCK_DGRAM parameters • Hunt for hardcoded ICMP sequence number 1234 and invalid ICMP Code 1 • Flag processes with non-existent executable paths and root-level daemon spoofing
Use Rapid7's triage script to identify active BPF filters and validate masqueraded processes. Full YARA rules and Suricata signatures available in the research.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039750364480602273

item.title: ghostsurf: From NTLM Relay to Browser Session Hijacking
item.description:
New tool release: ghostsurf enables browser-based access to NTLM-protected web applications through relayed sessions, solving fundamental limitations in ntlmrelayx's HTTP SOCKS proxy for enterprise password vaults and internal web apps.
Technical breakdown: • Addresses ntlmrelayx browser incompatibility: concurrent connection conflicts, corrupted HTTP streams, confusing basic auth prompts requiring domain/user format • Implements mutex-style thread locking to serialize browser requests through single authenticated relay socket • Discovers undocumented Windows kernel-mode auth behavior: anonymous resource requests reset auth context, triggering re-authentication • Probe-first strategy (-k flag) sends anonymous requests to public resources, preserves relay socket for authenticated paths only • Auto-selects single sessions, provides HTML picker for multiple relayed users with cookie-based session binding
Key targets: Enterprise password managers (CyberArk, Passwordstate, Secret Server), SCCM, internal IIS sites with Windows Authentication-only (no anonymous)
Perfect for accessing web apps where cookie theft fails due to IIS kernel-mode authentication. Use -k flag by default to prevent silent session death on IIS targets.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039735465331347463

item.title: vSphere and BRICKSTORM Malware: A Defender's Guide
item.description:
Mandiant's comprehensive analysis reveals BRICKSTORM malware campaign exploiting vSphere infrastructure, establishing undetected persistence at the hypervisor layer where traditional EDR agents cannot operate.
Technical breakdown: • Targets VMware vCenter Server Appliance (VCSA) and ESXi hypervisors running on Photon Linux OS • Exploits weak identity architecture and limited visibility gaps rather than product vulnerabilities • Persistence achieved through /etc/rc.local.d modifications and startup script tampering • BRICKSTEAL credential harvester intercepts passwords in memory during administrative sessions • Campaign active for average 393 days dwell time, operating beneath guest OS security controls
Attack methodology: • Phase 1: Compromise VCSA through weak authentication (vsphere.local accounts lack MFA support) • Phase 2: Leverage vpxuser account to pivot from management plane to ESXi host shell access • Phase 3: Establish persistence via startup script modification and binary injection using sed commands • Phase 4: Data exfiltration through VM cloning/export capabilities bypassing OS-level permissions
DFIR artifacts and detection opportunities: • Enable auditd remote logging: grep "execpriv\|privileged-priv-change" in SIEM for privilege escalation • Configure AIDE file integrity monitoring for /root/.ssh, /etc/rc.local.d, and /lib64 directories • Monitor VmClonedEvent and VmNetworkAdapterAddedEvent in vCenter logs for unauthorized VM operations
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039720224354865214

item.title: New Whitepaper: Stealthy BPFDoor Variants are a Needle That Looks Like Hay
item.description:
Rapid7 uncovers 7 new BPFDoor variants targeting global telecom infrastructure. New httpShell and icmpShell variants use kernel-level packet filters and stateless C2 routing to bypass enterprise security stacks.
Key technical details: • httpShell uses "magic ruler" padding (offset 26 bytes) to survive WAF/proxy interference, leverages kernel decapsulation of GRE/GTP tunnels • icmpShell implements PID-bound dynamic BPF filters, RC4 encryption with "icmp" key, terminates after 12s inactivity for stealth • Hidden IP (HIP) field enables stateless C2 - malware responds to source IP when flag set to 255.255.255.255, no hardcoded infrastructure needed • ICMP relay transforms infected machines into invisible network routers using hardcoded sequence 1234 and invalid ICMP code 1 • Multi-protocol variants spawn 3 threads monitoring TCP/UDP/ICMP simultaneously for C2 resilience • Active beacon variant masquerades as NTP-over-SSL, contacts domains like ntpussl.instanthq[.]com for guaranteed persistence
MITRE mapping includes T1205 (Traffic Signaling), T1090 (Proxy), T1572 (Protocol Tunneling), T1001 (Data Obfuscation via ICMP headers)
Hunt for AF_PACKET sockets with BPF filters attached, processes with non-existent executable paths, and ICMP traffic with hardcoded sequence 1234. Full YARA rules and triage script available in Rapid7's repository.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039705210072014999

item.title: An overview of ransomware threats in Japan in 2025 and early detection insights from Qilin cases
item.description:
Japan 🇯🇵 experienced 134 ransomware incidents in 2025, up 17.5% from 2024, with Qilin ransomware dominating at 16.4% of all attacks. Manufacturing remained the top target while healthcare emerged as a strategic focus for maximum disruption impact.
Key findings from Cisco Talos analysis: • Qilin affiliates leverage stolen credentials from Telegram/breach forums for initial access, showing operational maturity with step-by-step attack manuals • New EDR killer malware disables 300+ security solutions, implements geo-fencing to exclude post-Soviet countries (suggesting regional actor ties) • Average 6-day dwell time from initial compromise to ransomware deployment across analyzed cases • SMEs comprised 57% of victims vs 17% large enterprises, consistent with opportunistic targeting • Manufacturing (28%) and automotive (8%) sectors most impacted, with healthcare prioritized for operational disruption
Detection opportunities during pre-ransomware phase include monitoring net user account creation commands, correlating multiple suspicious events within 15-minute windows, and flagging activity outside business hours to reduce false positives.
Hunt for unusual administrative account creation patterns and cross-reference with known Qilin IOCs. Full Sigma rules, YARA signatures, and correlation logic available in the Talos report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039689934697558485

item.title: You’re Not Supposed To ShareFile With Everyone (Progress ShareFile Pre-Auth RCE Chain CVE-2026-2699 & CVE-2026-2701)
item.description:
Progress ShareFile Storage Zone Controller hit with critical pre-auth RCE chain. CVE-2026-2699 authentication bypass combined with CVE-2026-2701 RCE enables complete compromise of 30,000+ internet-facing instances.
Technical breakdown: • CVE-2026-2699: Execution After Redirect (CWE-698) in /ConfigService/Admin.aspx - Response.Redirect() called with 'false' parameter allows code execution to continue after redirect • CVE-2026-2701: File upload path manipulation + ZIP extraction abuse enables webshell deployment to webroot directory • Attack chain: Bypass auth → modify Zone configuration → change storage path to webroot → upload ZIP with ASPX webshell → extract via unzip=true parameter • Affects StorageCenter 5.x branch (ASP.NET), patched in version 5.12.4 released March 10, 2026
DFIR artifacts: • HTTP 302 responses with >10,000 character body from /ConfigService/Admin.aspx • Modifications to Zone configuration files and storage repository settings • Unusual ZIP uploads to /upload.aspx with unzip=true parameter • ASPX files extracted to webroot directories (C:\inetpub\wwwroot\ShareFile\)
Hunt for HTTP requests to /ConfigService/Admin.aspx returning 302 with large response bodies, and monitor for unexpected file uploads to administrative endpoints.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039674874525061235

item.title: UAT-10608: Inside a large-scale automated credential harvesting operation targeting web applications
item.description:
UAT-10608 cluster exploits React2Shell (CVE-2025-55182) to harvest credentials from 766 Next.js applications via automated NEXUS Listener framework. Operation captured 10,120 files including AWS keys, SSH private keys, and live Stripe tokens across multiple cloud providers.
Technical breakdown: • CVE-2025-55182: Pre-auth RCE in React Server Components via malicious serialized payloads to Server Function endpoints • Multi-phase harvesting script drops to /tmp/.{random}.sh, executes via nohup, exfiltrates to C2:8080 • Collection phases: environ, jsenv, ssh, tokens, history, cloud_meta, k8s, docker, cmdline, proc_all • NEXUS Listener v3 web GUI provides searchable database of stolen credentials with statistics dashboard • 91.5% had database creds, 78.2% SSH keys, 25.6% AWS creds, 11.4% live Stripe keys
Attack chain enables lateral movement via compromised SSH keys, cloud infrastructure takeover through IAM credentials, and supply chain attacks via package registry tokens. Automated scanning likely uses Shodan/Censys to identify vulnerable Next.js deployments.
Hunt for nohup processes spawning from /tmp/ with dot-prefixed randomized names and unexpected HTTP callbacks to port 8080. Full IOC list available on Cisco Talos GitHub.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039659848497799481

item.title: Qilin EDR killer infection chain
item.description:
Qilin ransomware deploys sophisticated multi-stage EDR killer targeting 300+ security products. Advanced loader uses SEH/VEH-based obfuscation and kernel manipulation to completely disable endpoint detection systems.
Technical breakdown: • Stage 1: Malicious msimg32.dll side-loaded via DLL hijacking, implements slot-policy table for syscall evasion and Halo's Gate technique • Stage 2-3: Complex VEH-based control flow obfuscation, overwrites ExitProcess IAT entry, maps payload into shell32.dll memory space • Stage 4: EDR killer loads rwdrv.sys (abused ThrottleStop driver) for physical memory R/W, hlpdrv.sys for process termination (T1562.001) • Kernel manipulation: Unregisters EDR callbacks for process/thread/image events, overwrites CiValidateImageHeader with ArbPreprocessEntry • Geo-fencing excludes post-Soviet countries, requires admin privileges for driver loading
Attack chain systematically blinds behavioral detection before ransomware deployment. Hunt for msimg32.dll side-loading, unsigned drivers in system directories, and unexpected ExitProcess IAT modifications.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039644788887318919

item.title: Axios Just Got Weaponized — And Your npm install Pulled the Trigger
item.description:
Supply chain attack compromises axios npm package via malicious dependency injection. Attackers gained maintainer credentials to push trojanized versions that executed cross-platform RATs during installation.
Key technical details: • Malicious axios@1.14.1 and @0.30.4 added plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 dependency with postinstall script executing node setup.js automatically • Multi-stage payload drops platform-specific RATs: Windows uses PowerShell + VBScript, Linux deploys Python script to /tmp/ld.py, macOS drops Mach-O binary to /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond • C2 infrastructure: sfrclak[.]com:8000/6202033 with HTTP POST beacons using User-Agent "mozilla/4.0 (compatible; msie 8.0)" • Windows persistence via HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\MicrosoftUpdate registry key • Self-deletion after execution: fs.unlink(__filename) removes loader, renames package.md to package.json to appear legitimate
Hunt for recent axios installations, monitor %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe, /tmp/ld.py, and /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond file creation. Check registry Run keys for MicrosoftUpdate entries and network connections to sfrclak[.]com.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039614585079001308

item.title: Hooked on Linux: Rootkit Detection Engineering
item.description:
Elastic Security Labs exposes fundamental flaws in static rootkit detection, showing how trivial binary modifications evade traditional signatures. Research introduces comprehensive behavioral detection framework for Linux rootkits across userland, kernel, eBPF, and io_uring attack vectors.
Key findings: • Static detection fails catastrophically: single null byte addition drops detection rates from 36/66 to 21/66 engines for Azazel rootkit • Behavioral indicators provide reliable detection: LD_PRELOAD abuse, shared object creation in /tmp/.hidden/, LKM loading via init_module/finit_module syscalls • Emerging attack vectors require new telemetry: eBPF rootkits using bpf_probe_write_user helper, io_uring-based stealth techniques bypassing syscall monitoring • Persistence mechanisms span multiple layers: shell config modification, udev rule abuse (/etc/udev/rules.d/), kernel module autoload via /etc/modules-load.d/ • Defense evasion includes process masquerading as kworker/kthreadd, timestomping via touch command, log clearing through dmesg -c
Hunt for unusual shared objects in ephemeral paths, monitor high-numbered kill signals (32+), and implement Auditd rules for init_module/finit_module syscalls. Full detection rules and YARA signatures available in Elastic's detection-rules repository.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039539284336591359

item.title: Anthropic Claude Code Leak
item.description:
Anthropic Claude Code source leak weaponized: Threat actors distribute Vidar stealer and GhostSocks proxy malware through fake GitHub repositories masquerading as the leaked AI agent codebase.
Key technical details: • Original leak: 59.8MB JavaScript source map exposed 513K lines of TypeScript code via npm package @anthropic-ai/claude-code v2.1.88 • Malicious repos rank high in Google searches for "leaked Claude Code" • Dropper: ClaudeCode_x64.exe (Rust-based) delivers Vidar v18.7 + GhostSocks proxy tools • IOCs: Archive hashes d8256fbc62e85dae85eb8d4b49613774, C2s steamcommunity[.]com/profiles/76561198721263282 and 147.45.197[.]92:443 • Attack leverages supply chain confusion - thousands of GitHub forks create perfect cover for trojanized versions
DFIR impact: • Exposed agent hooks, MCP integrations, OAuth flows enable precise exploitation of legitimate Claude deployments • Perfect timing overlap with malicious Axios npm supply chain attack (March 31) • Developer workstation compromise via malicious dependencies in fake source builds
Hunt for recent GitHub clones of "leaked-claude-code" repos, unusual Rust executables, and outbound connections to Steam community profiles from developer workstations.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039523871116628135

item.title: How we caught the Axios supply chain attack
item.description:
Elastic Security Labs catches massive Axios supply chain attack using AI-powered package monitoring tool. DPRK-attributed actors compromised npm maintainer accounts, injecting cross-platform malware via phantom dependency affecting one of npm's most popular packages.
Key technical details: • Attackers compromised maintainer accounts, published malicious Axios v1.14.1 and v0.30.4 with phantom dependency "plain-crypto-js" • Malware deployed via postinstall hooks, featuring cross-platform RAT with steganography in WAV files • Part of larger TeamPCP campaign that previously hit Trivy, LiteLLM, and Telnyx packages using stolen CI/CD credentials • Detection via real-time diff analysis of package changes using Cursor AI agent with custom prompts
Attack chain: • Compromised npm accounts → changed email to ProtonMail → published malicious versions • Added phantom dependency instead of direct code injection to evade detection • Postinstall scripts deployed persistent malware disguised as msbuild.exe on Windows • C2 server overwhelmed by infection volume, limiting impact scope
DFIR artifacts: • Elastic Defend alerts on infected hosts provided initial detection signals • Package installation logs showing phantom dependency downloads • Postinstall script execution evidence in process telemetry
Implement package soak time with `npm config set min-release-age 7` to delay updates. Full technical analysis and detection rules available from Elastic Security Labs.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039508768011309187

item.title: Mitigating the Axios npm supply chain compromise
item.description:
North Korean 🇰🇵 Sapphire Sleet compromises Axios npm packages with malicious dependency injection. Two versions (1.14.1, 0.30.4) deployed cross-platform RATs targeting 70M+ weekly downloads on March 31, 2026.
Attack mechanics: • Malicious plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 dependency executes automatically via post-install hook (T1195.002) • C2 domain sfrclak[.]com:8000 serves OS-specific payloads via single endpoint • Windows: PowerShell RAT with registry persistence (HKCU Run key), drops %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe • macOS: Native binary /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond masquerades as Apple component • Linux: Python payload /tmp/ld.py launched detached via nohup
DFIR artifacts: • Registry: HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\MicrosoftUpdate • File paths: %TEMP%\6202033.vbs, %PROGRAMDATA%\system.bat, /tmp/ld.py • Network: POST requests to packages.npm.org/product[0-2] as OS identifiers • Process chains: npm install → node setup.js → platform-specific stagers
Hunt for npm post-install processes spawning curl/PowerShell with network connections to port 8000, and search node_modules for plain-crypto-js packages. Full IOC list and hunting queries in the Microsoft report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039493773718077787

item.title: Prioritizing Alerts Triage with Higher-Order Detection Rules
item.description:
Elastic Security Labs reveals Higher-Order Rules methodology for correlating multiple alerts across endpoints, network, and observability data. Production testing shows 65 atomic rules generate 8,000 daily alerts, reduced to ~30 prioritized correlations.
Key technical implementation: • ES|QL queries correlate by shared entities (host.id, user, IP, process.entity_id) with temporal logic and prevalence filtering • Cross-domain detection combines endpoint + network (PANW/FortiGate), endpoint + email, and security + observability metrics • "Newly observed" rules surface first-time alerts within 5-7 day lookback windows using DATE_DIFF functions • Process tree correlation via process.Ext.ancestry field identifies related malicious activity across process chains
Detection patterns: • Entity-based correlation: Multiple Elastic Defend alerts (≥3 unique rules OR ≥2 event codes) on same host in 24h window • Network enrichment: JOIN endpoint alerts with firewall C2 detections using source.ip = host.ip correlation • Observability fusion: CPU spike metrics + memory signature alerts = high-confidence cryptominer detection • Lateral movement: Alert correlation where alert_1.host.ip = alert_2.source.ip
Hunt for multiple distinct security alerts converging on the same host, user, or process within short timeframes. Full ES|QL correlation queries and 30+ detection rules available in the research.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039478482908971081

item.title: Frequently Asked Questions About the Axios npm Supply Chain Attack by North Korea-Nexus Threat Actor UNC1069
item.description:
North Korea 🇰🇵-nexus UNC1069 compromised the axios npm package (100M+ weekly downloads) for three hours on March 31, delivering WAVESHAPER.V2 cross-platform RAT to potentially millions of developer environments worldwide.
Attack breakdown: • Compromised @jasonsaayman maintainer account, changed email to ifstap@proton.me, used long-lived npm token to bypass legitimate GitHub Actions workflow • Injected malicious dependency "plain-crypto-js" with postinstall hook executing SILKBELL dropper (reversed Base64 + XOR cipher, key: "OrDeR_7077") • WAVESHAPER.V2 deployed platform-specific payloads: macOS binary to /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond, Windows PowerShell to %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe, Linux Python to /tmp/ld.py • C2 beacons every 60 seconds to sfrclak[.]com:8000 using Base64 JSON with IE8/XP User-Agent spoofing • Anti-forensic cleanup: deletes dropper, replaces package.json with clean stub post-infection
StepSecurity detected C2 contact in 12,000+ projects. Hunt for hardcoded artifacts by platform and block C2 domain. Full IOCs, YARA rules, and detection rules available in GTIG report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039463565472788947

item.title: Frequently Asked Questions About the Axios npm Supply Chain Attack by North Korea-Nexus Threat Actor UNC1069
item.description:
North Korea 🇰🇵-nexus UNC1069 compromised axios npm package (100M+ weekly downloads) for 3 hours on March 31, delivering WAVESHAPER.V2 cross-platform RAT. Malicious versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 deployed backdoors to potentially hundreds of thousands of developer systems.
Key technical details: • Attacker compromised @jasonsaayman npm account, bypassed GitHub Actions OIDC via long-lived classic token • Malicious dependency "plain-crypto-js" uses postinstall hook executing SILKBELL dropper with dual encoding (reversed Base64 + XOR key "OrDeR_7077") • WAVESHAPER.V2 payloads: macOS binary (/Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond), Windows PowerShell (%PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe), Linux Python (/tmp/ld.py) • C2 beacons every 60s to sfrclak[.]com:8000 using Base64 JSON, supports kill/rundir/runscript/peinject commands • Anti-forensic cleanup deletes dropper, restores clean package.json from stub file
DFIR artifacts: • Registry persistence: HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run "MicrosoftUpdate" • Hardcoded User-Agent: Internet Explorer 8 on Windows XP • Socket.dev detected compromise within 6 minutes, full IOC list and YARA rules available from GTIG

item.title: CVE-2025-53521: F5 BIG-IP APM Flaw Reclassified as Unauthenticated RCE
item.description:
CVE-2025-53521: Critical F5 BIG-IP APM vulnerability reclassified from DoS to unauthenticated RCE (CVSS 9.8). Initially dismissed as service disruption, now confirmed as pre-auth code execution with active exploitation.
Key technical details: • Affects BIG-IP APM when access policy configured on virtual server • Vulnerable versions: 15.1.0-15.1.10, 16.1.0-16.1.6, 17.1.0-17.1.2, 17.5.0-17.5.1 • Exploitation via crafted traffic to APM-enabled virtual servers • CISA KEV-listed with confirmed active exploitation campaigns • Memory-only webshell behavior complicates artifact detection
IOCs to hunt: • Suspicious files: /run/bigtlog.pipe, /run/bigstart.ltm • Modified system binaries: /usr/bin/umount, /usr/sbin/httpd • iControl REST activity from localhost • HTTP 201 responses with CSS content-type camouflage • SELinux disable attempts
Impact assessment required: • Priority: Internet-facing virtual servers with APM policies • Cross-reference process behavior, config changes, audit logs • Consider rebuild over cleanup if compromise window unclear
Hunt for unusual /mgmt/shared/identified-devices/config/device-info access and correlate multiple indicators since file-based IOCs may be absent.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039312596826755394

item.title: North Korea’s Lazarus Group Behind the Axios npm Supply Chain Attack
item.description:
North Korea's 🇰🇵 Lazarus Group compromised the axios JavaScript library—downloaded 100M+ times weekly—injecting WAVESHAPER.V2 backdoor via supply chain attack. Three-hour exposure window on March 31 affected 80% of cloud environments with potential for massive credential theft and downstream attacks.
Key technical details: • Compromised maintainer account inserted malicious "plain-crypto-js" dependency into axios v1.14.1 and v0.30.4 • SILKBELL dropper uses postinstall hooks for silent execution, deploys platform-specific payloads • WAVESHAPER.V2 backdoor connects to sfrclak[.]com:8000/6202033 using anomalous IE8/WinXP user-agent string • Payloads drop to /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond (macOS), %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe (Windows), /tmp/ld.py (Linux) • Anti-forensic cleanup deletes dropper and reverts package.json modifications
Impact assessment: • 3% execution rate in affected environments during 3-hour window • Hundreds of thousands of stolen credentials enable future supply chain attacks, ransomware, crypto theft • Downstream risk to dependent packages extends blast radius significantly
Check systems for RAT artifacts at specified paths, audit CI/CD logs for March 31 00:21-03:20 UTC, rotate credentials where artifacts found, block sfrclak[.]com egress.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039267140730073386
